Claude Code Feb 2026 Research: Top 5 Actionable Framework Improvements for Torus (17 gates, 33 skills, 13 hooks). (1) Hook JSON Decision Format (Low Effort): Exit 0 + {\"hookSpecificOutput\":{\"permissionDecision\":\"deny/warn\",\"reason\":\"...\"}} enables graduated escalation instead of binary block. Can enhance PreToolUse gates with graduated warnings before full blocks. (2) Agent-based Hooks (High Effort, Critical): Spawn 50-120s agents inside hooks for semantic verification (e.g., Gate 3 test validation, Gate 5 proof checking). Torus currently uses command/prompt hooks; agents would enable deeper codebase reasoning. (3) Agent Teams (Medium Effort): Opus 4.6 now supports 5-6 task-per-teammate teams. Orchestrate gate discussions, skill validation, and fix prioritization across 3-5 mini-agents. TeammateIdle/TaskCompleted hooks control workflow. (4) MCP Lazy Loading (Low-Medium Effort): 85% context reduction by lazy-loading MCP servers. Scale to 100+ MCP tools without bloating context. Use MCPSearch for on-demand tool resolution. (5) PostToolUseFailure Async Hooks (Medium Effort): Structured failure diagnosis → auto-recovery → causal chain recording. Extends existing causal-chain pattern with failure closure loop. All patterns documented in Claude Code docs Feb 2026.

All HTML files use GitHub dark theme (#0d1117 bg, #58a6ff accent). All data verified from source (16 gates, 39 skills, 7 agents, 48 shared modules, 1533 tests, 1086 memories).

Key Design Patterns in the Framework:
1. Memory-first pattern: Gates 4, 7, 8 enforce querying memory before code edits (different time windows: 5min, 3min, 2min)
2. Verification pipeline: Edit -> pending_verification -> test/command run -> verified_fixes -> save to memory (Gate 6 nudge)
3. Per-session state isolation: Each agent gets state_{session_id}.json preventing cross-contamination in team workflows
4. Sideband memory timestamp: MCP server writes .memory_last_queried file so enforcer detects memory queries that bypass hook tracking. Team members only check own state (no sideband leakage).
5. Fail-closed for safety: Tier 1 gate crashes = tool blocked. Tier 2/3 gate crashes = logged, allowed through.
6. Atomic state writes: write-to-tmp + os.replace() prevents corruption
7. files_read cap: MAX_FILES_READ=200 prevents unbounded state growth
8. Session lifecycle: Boot clears all state -> agents create own state -> PostToolUse updates -> PreToolUse checks

Design: Plugin Architecture for Torus Framework (Session 298)

Three options explored:
- Option A (RECOMMENDED): Toggle Layer — manifest.json + dynamic gate_registry + try/except enforcer imports. Small effort, low risk. Setup.py rewrites gate_registry.py and settings.json.
- Option B: Registry-Driven — extend existing plugin_registry.py (656 lines) to manage gate packs. Medium effort.
- Option C: Pack Files — self-describing extension directories with pack.json. Large effort.

Key findings from dependency analysis:
- Only 3 hard breaks exist (all in enforcer.py lines 35-37): gate_timing, security_profiles, domain_registry
- Everything else already has try/except guards
- Zero cross-gate state coupling issues
- Core is ~15K lines (16% of Python), extensions ~65K (68%), tests ~30K (32%)

Decision: Start with Option A, evolve to B or C as needed. Design doc at docs/plans/plugin-architecture.md.

- medium/low → GateResult.warn(...)

Pi-agent extension API capabilities discovered for Torus integration: (1) promptGuidelines — per-tool behavioral rules injected into system prompt Guidelines section every turn. Use on search_knowledge ("Always query memory before editing") and remember_this ("Save every fix/decision to memory after verifying"). (2) promptSnippet — one-liner in Available tools section. Deep research on Karpathy/autoresearch (2026-03-09). Full architecture documented:

WHAT IT IS: Autonomous ML research loop. Agent edits train.py (single-GPU GPT training on nanochat), runs 5-min experiments, checks val_bpb metric (lower=better), commits if improved / git-reset if not, loops forever until human interrupts.

FILES: prepare.py (read-only: data prep, tokenizer, evaluate_bpb), train.py (agent-editable: GPT model + Muon+AdamW optimizer + training loop), program.md (human-editable agent instructions).

PROGRAM.MD STRUCTURE: Setup section (branch creation, file reading, results.tsv init), Experimentation section (constraints, simplicity criterion), Output format (val_bpb, training_seconds, peak_vram_mb, mfu_percent, total_tokens_M, num_params_M, depth), Logging (5-col TSV: commit, val_bpb, memory_gb, status, description), Experiment loop (LOOP FOREVER: git state → modify → commit → run > run.log → grep results → log → advance/reset).

KEY DESIGN: (1) NEVER STOP — agent runs indefinitely, no asking human. (2) Commit BEFORE running (unlike our skill which commits after). (3) 5-col TSV with memory_gb column (we have 4-col). (4) Crash handling: read traceback from run.log, attempt fix if dumb error, skip if fundamentally broken. (5) No stop conditions — pure infinite loop. (6) Timeout: kill if >10min. (7) Simplicity criterion explicitly weighted. (8) Output redirected to run.log — never floods context. (9) git reset (not git checkout -- .) for discard.

Circuit breaker module implemented at /home/crab/.claude/hooks/shared/circuit_breaker.py.

States: CLOSED (normal), OPEN (reject calls), HALF_OPEN (recovery probe).
Defaults: failure_threshold=5, recovery_timeout=60s, success_threshold=2.
Persistence: /dev/shm/claude-hooks/circuit_breaker.json (ramdisk), fallback to ~/.claude/hooks/.circuit_breaker.json.
Thread-safe: module-level threading.Lock + atomic rename via tempfile.

Public API:
  record_success(service, ...)
  record_failure(service, ...)
  is_open(service) -> bool         # True = reject calls
  get_state(service) -> str        # "CLOSED" | "OPEN" | "HALF_OPEN"
  get_all_states() -> Dict         # snapshot of all tracked services
  reset(service)                   # force back to CLOSED

All functions wrapped in try/except — fail-open, never crash caller.
State transitions: CLOSED → OPEN (failures >= threshold), OPEN → HALF_OPEN (timeout elapsed), HALF_OPEN → CLOSED (successes >= success_threshold), HALF_OPEN → OPEN (any failure).

Smoke test: 16/16 assertions pass. Full framework suite: 1124 pass (pre-existing 76 failures, no regressions).

ARXIV RESEARCH: Agent0 (arXiv:2511.16043) - Self-Evolving Agents from Zero Data via Tool-Integrated Reasoning

Key Contribution: Symbiotic competition between two agents from the same base LLM: (1) Curriculum Agent proposes increasingly challenging frontier tasks, (2) Executor Agent learns to solve them. External tool integration enhances executor, which pressures curriculum agent to create more complex tool-aware tasks — creating a self-reinforcing cycle. No human-annotated data required.

Performance: +18% mathematical reasoning, +24% general reasoning on Qwen3-8B-Base.

Torus Framework Applicability:
- Escalating Difficulty Mechanism: Gates could auto-increase strictness as system capabilities improve (not static thresholds)
- Self-Pressuring Standards: As Torus passes gates more reliably, gates become more stringent — preventing quality plateaus
- Tool-Integrated Validation: Specialized validators (security, perf) evolve alongside the system being validated

Implementable Pattern: Gate Curriculum — track gate pass rates over rolling window. When pass rate > 95% for 50+ evaluations, auto-increase gate threshold (e.g., gate min coverage from 80%→85%). Logged to LanceDB as a gate_evolution event. Mirrors Agent0's curriculum escalation without external data.

URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.16043

Framework Comparison: Megaman vs thepopebot (Session 32, 2026-02-14).

thepopebot is NOT a Claude Code customization framework — it's an autonomous agent infrastructure built on Node.js + Docker + GitHub Actions + Pi coding agent. Fundamentally different problem domain.

Architecture: Event Handler (Express/Telegram) → GitHub Actions → Docker container (Pi agent + Chromium). Two-layer: orchestration + execution.

Token overhead: thepopebot CLAUDE.md ~4,160 tokens (3.15x larger than Megaman's 1,321). But it documents infrastructure (Docker, GitHub Actions, webhooks), not behavioral rules.

Key differences:
- Execution: Event-driven containers (thepopebot) vs interactive prompts (Megaman)
- Memory: In-memory chat only (thepopebot) vs ChromaDB persistent (Megaman)
- Safety: ALLOWED_PATHS + env-sanitizer (thepopebot) vs 13 behavioral gates (Megaman)
- State: Git-as-state (thepopebot) vs LIVE_STATE.json + ChromaDB (Megaman)
- Testing: 0 tests (thepopebot) vs 901 tests (Megaman)
- UI: Telegram bot (thepopebot) vs CLI only (Megaman)

thepopebot strengths: production deployment, containerized, Telegram chat, browser automation, easy JSON config.
Megaman strengths: persistent memory, 13 quality gates, 901 tests, self-healing, causal chain tracking, token efficiency.

[chainovi/eclipse-agave #18] CRITICAL FINDING: "Bank-level marker consumption" is a MISNOMER. 

The bank has `is_emergency_unlocked()` method but it:
1. Only READS the marker state (checks owner==stake_program + lamports>0)
2. NEVER modifies/consumes the marker
3. Is NOT called anywhere in bank fee distribution logic
4. Is only used in integration tests (test_emergency_unlock_marker)

The ACTUAL marker consumption happens ONLY in the stake program:
- stake_instruction.rs Deactivate dispatch (line 232): `consume_emergency_marker(3, 0)`
- stake_instruction.rs GovernanceUnlock permanently locked (line 448): `consume_emergency_marker(1, 0)`
- stake_instruction.rs GovernanceUnlock passive (line 462): `consume_emergency_marker(1, 0)`
- stake_instruction.rs EarlyUnlock (line 422): `consume_emergency_marker(4, 0)`

consume_emergency_marker (stake_state.rs lines 415-433):
- Transfers marker's lamports to stake account
- Closes marker (lamports→0, effectively consuming it)
- This is ONE-TIME by design (lamports only transferred once, then 0)

The marker lifecycle:
1. Governance program creates PDA [b"emergency_unlock", target_stake_pubkey] (governance-owned)
2. write_execution_side_effects (line 584): governance calls set_owner to transfer ownership to stake program
3. Marker data stays ZERO (required by set_owner)
4. Stake program validates: owner==stake_program + lamports>0 + PDA derivation
5. Stake program consumes: transfer lamports to stake, zero marker (lamports=0 = consumed)

NO RACE CONDITION: Once marker is consumed (lamports=0), subsequent checks fail (lamports>0 test fails).
NO BANK-LEVEL CONSUMPTION: Bank never writes to marker, only reads its state for validation purposes (currently unused in fee logic).

Enforcer Hook System Design:
- File: /home/crab/.claude/hooks/enforcer.py
- Runs on PreToolUse (timeout 5s) and PostToolUse (timeout 3s)
- PreToolUse: Reads tool call JSON from stdin, loads per-session state, runs 8 gates in order. If any gate returns blocked=True, prints to stderr and exits code 1 (blocks the tool).
- PostToolUse: Tracks state — files read, memory queries, test runs, edits pending verification.
- Always-allowed tools skip all gates: Read, Glob, Grep, WebSearch, MCP memory tools, task management tools.
- Per-session state isolation: Each agent gets state_{session_id}.json to prevent cross-contamination.
- Atomic writes: write-to-tmp + os.replace() pattern prevents corruption.
- Fail-closed for Tier 1 gates: If a gate crashes, the tool call is blocked. Tier 2/3 errors are logged but allowed through.
- Shared utilities: shared/state.py (state load/save/cleanup), shared/gate_result.py (GateResult class)

Trading framework gate architecture decision: Neither pure bash (AKIRA) nor Claude hook gates (torus) are right for a trading framework. Trading gates should be Python functions embedded directly in the engine — no stdin, no exit codes, no shell overhead.

Why bash fails for trading: Can't compute portfolio correlation, Sharpe ratio, drawdown analysis, position sizing based on volatility. Trading risk management needs real computation (numpy, pandas), not regex + exit codes.

Why torus gates don't fit: Designed around Claude's hook protocol (JSON stdin, exit codes, GateResult, enforcer routing). Trading framework is a standalone engine, not a Claude session.

Architecture: TradingGate base class with check(order, portfolio, market) → GateResult. Three tiers:
- Tier 1 (hard block, fail-closed): max position size, daily loss limit, insufficient capital, market closed
- Tier 2 (block with override): no thesis logged, strategy not backtested, cooldown after loss, correlation too high  
- Tier 3 (advisory): unusually large order, trading outside hours, strategy underperforming

What to steal from each system:
- From AKIRA: binary block/allow (no warnings for trades), fail-closed, data-driven rules, compliance snapshots
- From torus: numbered testable gates, memory integration (learn from trades), causal chain (thesis→entry→outcome), tiered severity

Implementation: Python functions called inside the engine before executor sends orders. Same philosophy as torus (prevent mistakes before they happen) but completely different execution model (embedded, not shell hooks).

Created /home/crab/.claude/hooks/benchmark_gates.py — gate performance benchmark script.

Key results from first run (2026-02-20):
- All 15 active gates benchmarked successfully (0 load errors)
- 1000 iterations per gate, realistic allow-path scenarios
- Average p95 across all gates: 0.33ms
- Only 1 gate flagged as needing optimization: gate_11_rate_limit (p95=3.64ms)
  - Root cause: calls save_state() on every invocation (file I/O inside the gate)
- Fastest gates: gate_09 (p95=0.001ms), gate_06 (p95=0.003ms), gate_05 (p95=0.006ms)
- Pipeline estimate for Edit call (11 gates): p50=0.81ms, p95=4.24ms

Script design:
- Imports gates via importlib.import_module()
- One realistic scenario per gate (allow path, exercises main logic)
- Per-gate state is a shallow dict.update() copy of BASE_STATE
- Reports p50/p95/p99/min/max/mean per gate

## Multi-Agent Coordination: Hierarchical Orchestration Patterns (arxiv research, session268)

### Key Papers:

**AgentOrchestra** (arxiv:2506.12508) - Hierarchical Multi-Agent Framework
- Central planner orchestrates specialized sub-agents: web navigation, data analysis, file ops
- Continual adaptation: dynamically instantiates, retrieves, refines tools during execution
- TEA Protocol (Tool-Environment-Agent)
- Achieves 89.04% on GAIA benchmark (state-of-the-art)

**AdaptOrch** (arxiv:2602.16873, Feb 2026) - Task-Adaptive Topology Selection
- Dynamically selects among 4 canonical topologies: parallel, sequential, hierarchical, hybrid
- Selection based on task dependency graphs + empirically derived domain characteristics
- 12-23% improvement over static single-topology baselines
- KEY INSIGHT: No single topology is best — match topology to task dependency structure

**Multi-Agent Collaboration via Evolving Orchestration** (arxiv:2505.19591)
- Puppeteer paradigm: centralized orchestrator ("puppeteer") directs agents ("puppets")
- Orchestrator trained via RL to adaptively sequence and prioritize agents
- Enables flexible, evolvable collective reasoning

**OrchVis** (arxiv:2510.24937) - Hierarchical Orchestration + Human Oversight
- Hierarchical goal alignment + task assignment + conflict resolution
- Humans supervise without micromanaging each step

### Pattern for Torus:
- AdaptOrch insight critical: torus-loop.sh (sequential) vs torus-wave.py (parallel) should be selected dynamically based on task dependency graph, not predetermined
- 12-23% improvement from topology selection alone is significant

Git Worktrees explained and evaluated for torus-framework use:

WHAT: A worktree is a physical second checkout of the same repo in a different directory, sharing the same .git. Unlike branches (which are just pointers), worktrees let you have multiple branches checked out simultaneously without stashing/switching.

CLAUDE CODE INTEGRATION: Agent tool supports `isolation: "worktree"` — creates temp worktree, agent works in isolated copy, returns branch name if changes made, auto-cleans if no changes. New in 2.1.69: `worktree` field in hook event data exposes worktree metadata to hooks.

BEST USE CASE FOR US: Framework evolution (risky changes to live hooks/gates/shared modules). Agent edits in worktree can't break the live enforcer mid-session. Review diff, merge when satisfied.

PRACTICAL WORKFLOW: Agent refactors capability_registry.py in worktree → runs tests there → returns branch → we review git diff → merge or discard. No risk to live session.

WHEN NOT TO USE: Quick single-file edits, changes to files needed in current session (LIVE_STATE.json), hooks can't be tested "live" from worktree without extra setup.

REQUIRES: Agent teams feature currently disabled (CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=0). Worktrees work without teams but shine most with parallel agents.

CLAUDE_SESSION_ID in Hooks — Research Summary (March 2026):

1. NO `CLAUDE_SESSION_ID` ENVIRONMENT VARIABLE EXISTS (as of March 2026).
   - GitHub Issue #25642 is an open feature request for it. Status: OPEN, marked Stale/Duplicate. Not yet shipped.

2. SESSION_ID IS AVAILABLE IN THE STDIN JSON PAYLOAD — always.
   - Field: `session_id` (string) — present on ALL hook events, no exceptions.
   - Official docs confirm: "session_id: Current session identifier" is a common input field for every event.
   - The Torus framework already reads it: enforcer reads stdin JSON, state.py uses `load_state(session_id=...)` to key per-session state files.

3. NO VARIABLE SUBSTITUTION IN HOOK COMMANDS for session_id.
   - The `command` string in settings.json does NOT support `${CLAUDE_SESSION_ID}` interpolation.
   - Only `$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR` and `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` are substituted in command strings.
   - HTTP hook `headers` DO support `$VAR_NAME` interpolation, but ONLY for env vars listed in `allowedEnvVars`, and only if the env var already exists in the process environment.

4. WORKAROUND: SessionStart hook can extract session_id from stdin and write it to CLAUDE_ENV_FILE.
   - `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE` is a special env var available in SessionStart hooks.
   - Hook can: `SESSION_ID=$(echo "$INPUT" | jq -r '.session_id')` then `echo "export CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID=$SESSION_ID" > "$CLAUDE_ENV_FILE"`
   - After SessionStart completes, CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID becomes available in subsequent hook Bash environments.

5. TORUS CURRENT APPROACH: reads session_id from stdin JSON directly in Python hook scripts. No env var needed.
   - enforcer.py, tracker.py etc. all parse stdin JSON and extract `session_id` at the top of main().
   - State files named `state_{session_id}.json` — per-agent isolation already works.

Key distinction: session_id in stdin JSON = YES (always). CLAUDE_SESSION_ID as env var = NO (feature request only, not shipped).

## Multi-Agent Coordination: Task Decomposition Patterns (arxiv research, session268)

### Key Papers & Approaches:

**TDAG** (arxiv:2402.10178) - Dynamic Task Decomposition + Agent Generation
- Dynamically decomposes complex tasks → smaller subtasks → assigns each to specifically generated subagent
- Outperforms baselines on adaptability/context-awareness in complex scenarios

**DynTaskMAS** (arxiv:2503.07675) - Dynamic Task Graph + Async Parallel Execution
- Dynamic Task Graph Generator: intelligently decomposes tasks maintaining logical dependencies
- Asynchronous Parallel Execution Engine: optimizes resource via efficient task scheduling
- Maps well to torus-wave.py parallel waves architecture

**Modular Task Decomposition** (arxiv:2511.01149) - LLM-based hierarchical sub-tasks
- Converts NL task descriptions → unified semantic representations
- Breaks down via modular decomposition into hierarchical sub-tasks

**Learning Symbolic Task Decompositions** (arxiv:2502.13376)
- Learns optimal decomposition from model-free environment interactions
- Task-conditioned architecture: simultaneous optimal decomposition + agent policies
- Removes need for human-designed decomposition

### Pattern for Torus: 
- Use dependency graph to determine task execution order before spawning agents
- Async parallel execution where dependencies allow (already in torus-wave.py)

Deep analysis of compressed cross-agent transcript visibility feature. Proposed tool: get_teammate_context(agent_name, max_tokens=300) — reads teammate JSONL transcript, extracts last 5 actions + outcomes, returns ~200-300 tokens. Token cost: +30-40% memory-related usage but <2% of total session. Benefits: real-time freshness (no waiting for remember_this), duplicate work prevention, better dependent task coordination. Drawbacks: new failure modes (file locked, concurrent read), noise risk from uncompressed reasoning, ephemeral (session-only, doesn't replace memory for cross-session). Implementation: ~120 lines in memory_server.py, ~15 tests, zero risk to existing system. Verdict: worth building but low urgency — breaks even after preventing 1 duplicate investigation per session. Best for 3+ agent teams with dependent tasks.

TORUS SWARM PATTERN — Multi-agent orchestration via tmux (proven working session 368)

NO SCRIPT NEEDED. The main Claude session acts as orchestrator. Pattern:

1. TASK DECOMPOSITION: Split work into independent tasks, write each to /tmp/agentN-task.md
2. LAUNCH WORKERS:
   - Create tmux session: tmux new-session -d -s agentN -c /path/to/project
   - Launch claude: tmux send-keys -t agentN "claude --dangerously-skip-permissions --append-system-prompt 'Read /tmp/agentN-task.md and execute immediately.'" Enter
   - Kick start: tmux send-keys -t agentN "Execute the task now." Enter
3. MONITOR: tmux capture-pane -t agentN -p -S -200 | tail -30
4. STEER: If agent gets stuck, send guidance via tmux send-keys
5. VERIFY: Check git status, run builds, confirm changes are compatible

KEY LESSONS LEARNED:
- Do NOT use claude -p (buffers output, can't watch live, can't interact)
- Do NOT inline prompts in tmux send-keys (quotes break) — use task files instead
- Check which tmux session you're IN (tmux display-message -p '#S') before sending keys
- claude + claude1 can share the same pane (check with tmux list-panes -F '#{pane_pid}')
- Shared directory > worktrees for cooperative tasks (agents see each other's changes)
- Worktrees only needed for fully independent tasks or risky experiments
- Each worker inherits full framework (gates, memory, skills) automatically
- Memory MCP is the coordination bus — agents discover each other's work via search_knowledge
- Gates enforce discipline on workers (Gate 4 blocked until memory queried, Gate 14 forced build verification)
- Practical limit: 3-5 agents max before diminishing returns
- Agent 2 finished genesis config in ~8min, Agent 1 finished op-geth Go mods in ~8min (parallel)

## Multi-Agent Coordination: Actionable Patterns for Torus Framework (session268 synthesis)

### 6 Key Patterns for Improvement:

**1. Dynamic Topology Selection** (from AdaptOrch 2602.16873)
- Problem: torus-loop.sh (sequential) vs torus-wave.py (parallel) is currently predetermined
- Solution: Analyze task dependency graph first → select topology dynamically
- Expected gain: 12-23% performance improvement

**2. Explorer/Worker/Validator Triad** (from SwarmSys 2510.10047)
- Maps to Torus agent types: researcher=Explorer, builder=Worker, stress-tester=Validator
- Natural pipeline: explore → build → validate (with feedback loop back to explore)
- Emergent coordination — no central controller needed for this triad

**3. SOPs as Hallucination Reduction** (from MetaGPT 2308.00352)
- Torus PRP system already implements this concept
- Opportunity: formalize intermediate verification checkpoints between agent handoffs

**4. Async Task Graph Execution** (from DynTaskMAS 2503.07675)
- Current torus-wave.py runs in waves; opportunity to run truly async where dependencies allow
- Dynamic dependency tracking → unblock tasks as soon as their dependencies complete

**5. Communication Overhead Penalty** (from Agent-GSPO 2510.22477)
- Design reward/scoring to penalize excessive inter-agent messages
- Memory MCP as shared state reduces need for direct agent-to-agent messaging

**6. Population Evolution for System Design** (from SwarmAgentic 2506.15672)
- Super-evolve skill could maintain population of skill/gate variants, select best by fitness
- PSO-inspired: keep top performers, evolve via feedback-guided updates

### Papers Most Relevant to Torus: AdaptOrch, DynTaskMAS, SwarmSys, MetaGPT

ARXIV RESEARCH: AgentEvolver (arXiv:2511.10395) - Efficient Self-Evolving Agent System

Key Contribution: Three-mechanism self-evolution via LLM semantic understanding:
1. Self-Questioning: Curiosity-driven task generation in novel environments, reducing dependence on handcrafted datasets
2. Self-Navigating: Improves exploration efficiency through experience reuse and hybrid policy guidance
3. Self-Attributing: Differentiates rewards by assigning contribution-weighted signals to trajectory states/actions (not uniform rewards)

Key Advantage: Eliminates need for manually curated training data while improving exploration efficiency and sample utilization vs. traditional RL baselines.

Torus Framework Applicability:
- Self-Attributing = our causal chain tracking: different events in a chain have different causal weight for the outcome
- Self-Questioning maps to gate-generated improvement tasks (gate 11 already prunes; could also generate new gates)
- Experience reuse = memory-first pattern we already have

Implementable Pattern: Enhance causal chain tracking with contribution weights. When record_outcome() is called, backpropagate a credit score to each record_attempt() in the chain. Higher credit = this strategy step was decisive. Low credit = coincidental. Store in fix_outcomes table with a `contribution_weight` field. Surfaces which fix steps actually matter.

URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.10395

ARXIV RESEARCH: MetaAgent - Tool Meta-Learning (arXiv:2508.00271)

Key Contribution: "Learning-by-doing" paradigm where expertise develops through hands-on practice. When encountering knowledge gaps, generates natural language help requests routed to best tool. Continuously self-reflects and verifies answers after task completion, distilling experience into concise actionable text. Builds persistent knowledge base from tool-use history. No parameter updates required.

Torus Framework Applicability:
- Our gate system already does something like this: gate 4 enforces memory-first, gates verify claims
- Missing: automated distillation of "actionable text" from gate fire events
- MetaAgent's "answer verification" mirrors our PROOF BEFORE FIXED gate

Implementable Pattern: After each successful fix verified by Bash (resetting gate 5 counter), automatically call remember_this() with a structured summary: "Fix for [error]: [strategy that worked]. Verified by: [test command output hash]." This mirrors MetaAgent's post-task self-reflection without manual effort. Could be a Gate 5 post-allow hook that triggers memory writing.

URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.00271

Agent Zero Architecture (v0.9.8.2):
- Monologue loop (dual while True) in agent.py with Agent/AgentContext/AgentConfig
- Tools are JSON-in-text (not native function calling), parsed by extract_tools.py DirtyJson
- 24 built-in tools including code_execution, browser_agent, memory_save/load, call_subordinate, a2a_chat
- Memory: FAISS CPU + all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (384d), 3 areas (MAIN, FRAGMENTS, SOLUTIONS), similarity threshold 0.7
- History compression: token budget = ctx_length * 0.7, cascade compress (truncate → summarize → merge bulks)
- Extensions: 24 lifecycle hooks (tool_execute_before/after, before_main_llm_call, message_loop_start, etc.)
- NO safety gates, NO input validation, NO prompt injection defense
- Multi-agent: parent→child hierarchy, subordinates inherit AgentContext
- Config: usr/settings.json + .env, LiteLLM for model routing
- MCP client support, A2A protocol (fasta2a), Docker-based isolation
- Key insight: extensions are the primary injection point for custom gates

Torus has 17 active gates (1-7, 9-11, 13-19). Agent Zero has ZERO gates.
The extension hook system (tool_execute_before) is the natural integration point for gate-like enforcement.

ARXIV RESEARCH: SAGE - Self-evolving Agents with Reflective and Memory-augmented Abilities (arXiv:2409.00872)

Key Contribution: Combines self-reflection with memory augmentation. Agents reflect on past interactions, store distilled knowledge in memory, and retrieve it to improve future performance. Achieves 2.26X improvement on closed-source models and 57.7%–100% improvement on open-source models, with particularly large gains on smaller models.

Torus Framework Applicability:
- Directly validates our memory-first + causal chain approach
- The "particularly notable effects on smaller models" is relevant: gate logic quality improves proportionally more for less capable agents in our swarm
- Our current pattern: search_knowledge → use findings. SAGE adds a reflection step: AFTER completing task, compare outcome vs. retrieved memory, note discrepancies, update memory.

Implementable Pattern: Add a post-task reflection hook. After session_end, compare: (a) what memory said the fix would be, vs (b) what actually worked (from fix_outcomes). If discrepancy > threshold, generate a correction memory entry with type:correction tag. This closes the SAGE loop: Memory → Act → Reflect → Update Memory.

URL: https://arxiv.org/html/2409.00872v2

ARXIV RESEARCH: Agent-R (arXiv:2501.11425) - Training Language Model Agents to Reflect via Iterative Self-Training

Key Contribution: Iterative self-training framework enabling agents to reflect on-the-fly and correct erroneous actions. Key insight: enables "timely error correction" and prevents agents from getting stuck in error loops. Achieves +5.59% over baselines on 3 interactive environments.

Also relevant: Multi-Agent Reflexion (MAR, arXiv:2512.20845) — extends Reflexion to multi-agent setting with diverse reasoning personas + judge model that synthesizes critiques. Separates acting, diagnosing, critiquing, and aggregating. Prevents "shared blind spots" from single-agent self-reflection. HotPotQA +3 points, HumanEval +6.2 points.

Torus Framework Applicability:
- Agent-R's "prevents error loops" is directly relevant to Gate 6 deadlock issue (documented in MEMORY.md)
- MAR's separation of acting/diagnosing/critiquing maps to our agent/gate/mentor architecture
- Our mentor system is the "judge" in MAR terms — mentor verdicts synthesize critiques

Implementable Pattern: When gate 6 escalation fires repeatedly (deadlock risk), instead of just blocking, spawn a "reflection sub-agent" that diagnoses WHY the loop is occurring (like Agent-R's on-the-fly reflection). The reflection output is stored to LanceDB with type:error tag, and the gate 6 warn_count is only reset if the reflection identifies a genuine fix, not just a bypass.

URLs: https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.11425, https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.20845

ARXIV RESEARCH: JitRL - Just-In-Time Reinforcement Learning (arXiv:2601.18510) + Lifelong Learning Roadmap (arXiv:2501.07278)

JitRL Key Contribution: Training-free framework enabling frozen LLMs to continuously adapt at test time by directly optimizing logits. Gradient-free, works at inference time without any training loop. Enables continual learning in LLM agents without gradient updates.

Lifelong Learning Roadmap Key Contribution: First systematic survey of incorporating lifelong learning into LLM-based agents. Identifies 3 pillars: (1) Perception Module (multimodal inputs), (2) Memory Module (evolving knowledge store), (3) Action Module (grounded interactions). Core challenge: catastrophic forgetting — agents lose previously acquired knowledge when learning new skills.

Torus Framework Applicability (JitRL):
- Our gates run at inference time — JitRL's test-time adaptation pattern means gates could dynamically adjust based on in-session signal without retraining
- Practically: gate thresholds could be session-warm (tighter early in session when agent is "cold", relax as session track record improves)

Torus Framework Applicability (Lifelong):
- We already have the Memory Module (LanceDB) — the missing piece is explicit catastrophic forgetting prevention
- Our memory compaction (hooks/scripts/memory_compactor.py) is the forgetting mechanism — needs to be smarter: preserve high-value memories (golden zone) even when compacting

Implementable Pattern: Add a `preserve_score` field to LanceDB knowledge entries. High preserve_score entries survive compaction even when old. Score increases each time the memory is successfully retrieved and used (track in observations table). Memory compactor only deletes entries where preserve_score < threshold AND age > max_age.

URLs: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.18510, https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.07278

HyMem Research (arxiv:2602.13933, Feb 15 2026): "HyMem: Hybrid Memory Architecture with Dynamic Retrieval Scheduling" by Xiaochen Zhao, Kaikai Wang, Xiaowen Zhang, Chen Yao, Aili Wang.

ARCHITECTURE: Dual-granular storage — Level-1 (event-level summaries with context/time/location/participants) + Level-2 (raw dialogue text). Explicit links between levels enable backtracking. Two-tier retrieval: (1) Lightweight module — vector embed query, cosine similarity top-k on L1, low latency for simple queries. (2) Deep module — activated only when lightweight detects incompleteness (~22% of queries with k=10). Deep module: coarse semantic recall (top-N), LLM self-retrieval for semantic/causal dependencies, backtracks to L2 for generation. Reflection mechanism: completeness check, query decomposition into sub-problems for iterative retrieval rounds.

BENCHMARKS:
LOCOMO (gpt-4o-mini): HyMem overall 89.55% vs Full-Context 77.51% vs Zep (best baseline) 75.14%. Multi-hop: 88.16% vs 46.89% full-context. Token cost: 1.5k avg vs 21.4k full-context = 92.6% reduction.
LongMemEval-s: HyMem 75.00% vs LightMem 68.64% vs Full-Context 56.80%.

KEY DISTINCTION: Not episodic/semantic layers — it's summary/raw granularity layers with dynamic scheduling of which retrieval tier to use.

HyMem Research (arxiv:2602.13933, Feb 2026): "HyMem: Hybrid Memory Architecture with Dynamic Retrieval Scheduling" by Xiaochen Zhao et al.

ARCHITECTURE: Dual-granularity storage (NOT episodic/semantic — it's summary/raw granularity):
- L1: Event-level summaries (context, time, participants, general progression) — fast retrieval
- L2: Raw dialogue text per event — full fidelity for complex queries
- Explicit L1→L2 links for backtracking

TWO-TIER RETRIEVAL:
- Tier 1 (lightweight): cosine similarity over L1 summaries, answers from L1 context, evaluates completeness
- Tier 2 (deep, conditional): activated only when T1 detects incompleteness (~22% of queries when k=10). Coarse semantic recall → LLM self-retrieval → L2 raw text backtracking
- Reflection mechanism: if still unresolved, decomposes into sub-queries for multi-hop

BENCHMARK RESULTS (backbone: gpt-4o-mini):
LOCOMO: 89.55% overall vs 77.51% full-context vs 75.14% Zep (best baseline)
- Multi-hop: 88.16% vs 46.89% full-context (near-doubling)
- Single-hop: 85.15% vs 80.14% full-context
- Temporal: 92.98% vs 90.49% full-context
- Token usage: 1,500 vs 21,400 (92.6% reduction)
LongMemEval-s: 75.00% vs LightMem 68.64% vs full-context 56.80%

STRENGTHS: SOTA on both benchmarks; 92.6% token reduction while beating full-context; Tier 2 only 22% activation; elegant L1→L2 backtracking
WEAKNESSES: No write-time consolidation (events static after initial summarization); completeness detection is a choke point; no cross-session knowledge evolution; not a true episodic/semantic split — misses "what it means" abstraction

[zerobrain #?] CORRECTION on ZeroTor gate analysis:

1. "G13 overlaps with G02" — WRONG. G02 catches destructive shell commands (rm -rf). G13 catches Python file writes (open('/etc/passwd', 'w')). Different code patterns, different attack surface. They're complementary, not redundant.

2. ZeroBrain's safety_critical_files is a DIFFERENT gate from G13. G13 guards system paths (/etc, ~/.ssh). safety_critical_files guards application-level files (.env, Dockerfile, package.json, .pem, nginx.conf). ZeroTor has neither — this should be added as a new gate, not merged into G13.

3. "G06 only fires when agent uses causal chain" isn't a flaw — it's correct design. The causal chain IS the intended workflow. Dormancy when agent ignores it is an adoption problem, not a gate problem.

4. ZeroBrain's output scanner is underrated. G17 scans inputs going TO web tools. Output scanner scans results coming BACK. Different attack vector — adversarial webpage content can't be blocked at input time. ZeroTor needs this.

5. G05 vs G19: NOT exactly redundant. G05 is proactive (before failures), G19 reactive (after failures). But G05's write detection via regex is too noisy in Agent Zero's single-tool model. The concept is salvageable with a better signal ("N consecutive code executions with no test output" instead of "N write-pattern matches").

Final recommended actions: Remove G01, G05. Rename G13. Loosen G04 to 15-20min. Add: Loop Escape, Depth Limit, Token Budget, Safety Critical Files, Output Scanner. Result: 13→16 gates.

ARXIV RESEARCH: FLEX - Forward Learning from Experience (arXiv:2511.06449)

Key Contribution: Gradient-free continuous evolution via a hierarchical "experience library." 3 stages: (1) Extensive exploration via parallel/sequential sampling, (2) Experience library evolution by an updater agent consolidating successes and failures, (3) Guided reasoning by retrieving experiences to augment future problem-solving. Actor-Critic at base level; Hierarchical 3-tier library at meta level (Strategic principles → Reasoning patterns → Factual instances). Dual zones: golden zone (successes) and warning zone (failures).

Performance: +23% AIME25, +10% USPTO50k, +14% ProteinGym. Shows experience scaling laws (accuracy improves predictably with library size). Model-agnostic: weaker model libraries improve stronger models.

Torus Framework Applicability:
- Our LanceDB memory IS the experience library — we have semantic search but lack the dual golden/warning zone structure
- Actor-Critic maps directly to our gate-agent interaction: agent acts, gate critiques
- Hierarchical retrieval: Strategic principles → gate logic, Patterns → common fix strategies, Factual → specific error fixes

Implementable Pattern: Add a 'zone' field to LanceDB knowledge table: zone="golden" for successful fixes, zone="warning" for failed approaches. Modify search_knowledge to bias toward golden zone for current task type, surface warning zone for anti-patterns. Update memory_server.py remember_this to auto-classify based on outcome.

DECISION: Hybrid memory linking approach chosen for problem/fix co-retrieval. Design: (1) Tag convention — use resolves:MEMORY_ID in existing tags field, zero schema cost. (2) Server-side hint — when remember_this() is called with type:fix but no resolves:ID, response includes hint to link. (3) Server-side validation — resolves:ID is checked against existing memories, warns if invalid. (4) Search-time co-retrieval — search_knowledge() fetches and appends linked memories (resolves/resolved_by). (5) Bidirectional — saving with resolves:ID updates the target memory with resolved_by metadata. Implementation: ~40-50 lines in memory_server.py. Zero per-prompt token cost, +125 tokens/session dynamic. Self-teaching via hints, improves adoption over time. Chosen over New Parameter (+6,500/session, best consistency but expensive) and Tag Convention alone (zero cost but poor discoverability). Upgrade path to parameter exists if adoption plateaus.

ActMem (arxiv 2603.00026): "Bridging the Gap Between Memory Retrieval and Reasoning in LLM Agents." Authors: Xiaohui Zhang, Zequn Sun, Chengyuan Yang, Yaqin Jin, Yazhong Zhang, Wei Hu. Submitted Feb 4, 2026.

KEY IDEA: Transforms passive memory (flat retrieval) into active causal reasoning. Builds a Memory Knowledge Graph (KG) from dialogue history.

ARCHITECTURE (4 modules):
1. Memory Fact Extraction — LLM converts raw dialogue into atomic declarative sentences
2. Fact Clustering — single-pass incremental embedding clustering (cosine threshold τ=0.2) to group topic-related facts
3. Memory KG Construction — semantic edges (similarity threshold 0.3) + causal edges (PMI-based filtering τ=0.8)
4. Counterfactual-Based Retrieval — (a) initial vector similarity retrieval, (b) counterfactual reasoning generating commonsense consequences, (c) secondary retrieval using generated knowledge

BENCHMARK: ActMemEval dataset — 246 validated samples (from 300 generated), avg semantic similarity 0.232 vs 0.347 for LongMemEval. Evaluates logic-driven, causal, conflict-detection scenarios.

RESULTS (Table 1):
- DeepSeek-V3 backbone: 71.66% retrieval / 76.52% QA accuracy
- GPT-4o-mini backbone: 76.92% retrieval / 52.22% QA accuracy
- Outperforms LightMem by 14.78% retrieval + 12.55% QA (DeepSeek-V3)
- On LongMemEval: 75.60% QA accuracy, +6.96% over LightMem

BASELINES: NaiveRAG (84.86% retrieval but 61.54% QA — reveals retrieval-reasoning gap), Mem0, A-Mem, MemoryOS, LightMem

ABLATION: Removing counterfactual reasoning → ~9% retrieval accuracy drop. Causal edges critical for QA. Semantic edges critical for context completeness.

NOTE: No episodic/semantic layer split; uses a unified KG. No flat memory comparison in the traditional sense — NaiveRAG is the flat-retrieval baseline.

## Arxiv Survey: Adaptive Forgetting & Importance Scoring for LLM Agent Memory (2025-2026)

### Ebbinghaus Forgetting Curve Applied to AI Memory
- MemoryBank implements decay mechanism inspired by Ebbinghaus curve
- **Differential memory decay**: important memories (frequently accessed, semantically relevant) decay slower
- Forgetting = feature, not bug: prevents cognitive overload, maintains relevance, enables generalization

### MemOS/MemScheduler (arXiv 2507.03724)
- Importance scoring formula: **contextual similarity + access frequency + temporal decay + priority tags**
- 5 lifecycle states: Generated → Activated → Merged → Archived → Expired
- State transitions based on: access patterns, time decay, task labels
- Frequent KV patterns cached as "instant memory paths" for low-latency retrieval
- Outperforms mem0, LangMem, Zep, OpenAI-Memory especially on multi-hop + temporal reasoning

### FadeMem / Adaptive Forgetting
- Teaching AI to forget selectively improves recall of important info
- Meta-learning approaches proposed to adapt decay parameters per agent/context
- Low-relevance entries decay over time → free up space and attention

### Implications for Torus Framework (LanceDB, 1552 entries)
- Add access_count + last_accessed metadata to knowledge table entries
- Implement score = similarity + recency_weight - decay(time_since_access)
- Entries crossing low threshold → Archived state (kept but deprioritized)
- Consider priority tags in remember_this() calls for decay resistance

## Multi-Agent Coordination: Software Dev Frameworks + Role Assignment (arxiv research, session268)

### ChatDev (arxiv:2307.07924) - Communicative Agents for Software Development
- Waterfall model: Design → Code → Test → Document
- Multiple agents per phase with social roles: requirements analysts, programmers, test engineers, reviewers
- Chat Chain: divides each phase into smaller subtasks with multi-turn agent communication
- Communicative Dehallucination: agents request specific details before responding → reduces coding hallucinations
- Key: structured sequential pipeline with role specialization per phase

### MetaGPT (arxiv:2308.00352) - Meta Programming for Multi-Agent Collaboration  
- Encodes Standardized Operating Procedures (SOPs) into prompt sequences
- Assembly line paradigm: diverse roles (PM, architect, PM, engineer)
- Agents verify intermediate results → reduces cascading hallucinations
- Input: one-line requirement → Output: user stories, analysis, requirements, data structures, APIs, docs
- 85.9-87.7% Pass@1, 100% task completion rate on benchmarks

### Self-Resource Allocation (arxiv:2504.02051)
- LLMs allocate computational tasks considering cost, efficiency, performance
- Planner methods > orchestrator methods for concurrent actions

### Key Survey (arxiv:2501.06322) - Multi-Agent Collaboration Mechanisms
- Dimensions: actors, types, structures, strategies, coordination protocols
- Centralized paradigms: robustness vulnerabilities + computational bottlenecks at scale

### Pattern for Torus:
- SOPs from MetaGPT = Torus PRP system — structured task descriptions reduce agent hallucination
- ChatDev's dehallucination (request clarification before acting) maps to gate pre-checks
- Decentralized task allocation reduces single-point bottlenecks vs centralized orchestrator

Agent Zero Deep Dive Research (Session 83):

CORE: Agent Zero is a personal, organic agentic framework — prompt-driven, transparent, fully customizable. No hard-coded rails. Repo: github.com/agent0ai/agent-zero, 14.6k stars, 3k forks, MIT license, v0.9.8 (Feb 2026).

ARCHITECTURE: Layered — Web UI → WebSocket/HTTP → AgentContext/Agent/AgentConfig → Tools → Memory (FAISS) → External (MCP, APIs). Core loop is the "monologue loop" (agent.py:383-532): prepare prompts → invoke LLM → detect tools → execute → integrate results.

MEMORY: FAISS vector DB with 3 areas (MAIN, FRAGMENTS, SOLUTIONS). Smart consolidation via LLM. Embedding: sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (local) or API-based. Project-level isolation.

MULTI-AGENT: Hierarchical superior-subordinate model. User → Agent 0 → subordinates. Parallel execution, shared memory access.

TECH STACK: Python, Flask 3.0.3 async, LiteLLM (25+ LLM providers), FastMCP, Playwright, FAISS, React WebUI, Docker. Replaced LangChain with LiteLLM in v0.9.1.

SKILLS: Uses SKILL.md standard (portable across Claude Code, Cursor, etc.).

## Multi-Agent Coordination: Communication Reduction + Protocols (arxiv research, session268)

### Key Papers:

**Agent-GSPO** (arxiv:2510.22477) - Communication-Efficient Multi-Agent via RL
- Frames message generation as sequential action
- Directly optimizes communication-aware reward: task success MINUS communication overhead (token count + conversational turns)
- KEY: Explicit reward shaping reduces unnecessary chatter while preserving task performance

**Agent Interoperability Protocols Survey** (arxiv:2505.02279)
- 4 emerging protocols: MCP (Model Context Protocol), ACP (Agent Communication Protocol), A2A (Agent-to-Agent), ANP (Agent Network Protocol)
- MCP: tool/context integration | A2A: agent-to-agent coordination | ACP: async messaging | ANP: network-wide discovery

**G-Designer** (arxiv:2410.11782) - Graph Neural Network Topology Design
- Uses GNN to architect optimal multi-agent communication topologies
- Conceptualizes communication structure as learnable graph
- Bi-level optimization: determine communication graph + update architecture parameters

**CommFormer** (arxiv:2511.09171) - Efficient Communication in Large-Scale MARL
- Learnable communication graph in partially observable distributed environments
- Enables effective large-scale communication without all-to-all overhead

**CoDe** (arxiv:2501.05207) - Communication Delay-Tolerant Collaboration
- Dual alignment of intent and timeliness
- Handles latency/delay in distributed multi-agent systems

### Pattern for Torus:
- Current: agents communicate via task list + memory MCP (already low-overhead)
- Opportunity: reward agents for task completion with fewer inter-agent messages
- MCP already implements the "MCP" protocol from the survey — well-positioned

Research findings from Claude Code docs and self-improving agent papers (Session 163 Sprint-2):

1. **Hook JSON Decisions**: Gates can return `{"hookSpecificOutput":{"permissionDecision":"deny","reason":"..."}}` instead of binary exit 2. Enables graduated escalation: warn → ask → block. LOW effort to implement.

2. **Async PostToolUseFailure Hooks**: Chain failure diagnosis → auto-recovery → error recording. Medium effort. Extends causal chain.

3. **MCP Tool Search / Lazy Loading**: When tools exceed context %, use MCPSearch for on-demand loading. Scales to 100+ tools. ALREADY partially used (85% reduction noted earlier).

4. **Agent-based Hooks**: Spawn mini-agent inside hook (50 tool calls, 120s timeout). Deep semantic verification instead of simple command checks. HIGH effort but Critical value for Gate 3 and Gate 5.

5. **Preference-Based Reward Modeling**: Collect user feedback as pairwise preferences → train reward model → agent self-modifies prompts. Critical for systematic self-evolution. HIGH effort.

Top 3 for immediate implementation: Hook JSON decisions (low effort), async failure hooks (medium), agent-based hooks for Gate 5 (high but critical).

Sprint-2 Research Swarm Results (15 haiku agents, Session 163):

1. HOOKS PATTERNS: Three-tier composition (Command→Prompt→Agent), graduated permissions via hookSpecificOutput JSON, async hooks for non-blocking gates, deterministic enforcement over suggestions, context re-injection after compaction via SessionStart hooks.

2. SELF-HEALING FRAMEWORKS: Multi-agent optimization (Observability→Insight→Optimization→Meta agents), autonomous retraining loops (trajectory sampling→reflection→prompt evolution), RepairAgent tool-based repair ($0.14/bug), Perception-Reasoning-Action cycle, lifelong agentic systems with optimizers.

3. AGENT ORCHESTRATION: Hierarchical supervisor pattern (17% efficiency gain), affinity-based dynamic allocation (23-28% boost), group chat + maker-checker consensus, sequential+concurrent hybrid staging, event-driven resource manager (90% token reduction via agent contracts).

4. QUALITY GATES: Progressive enforcement (soft→hard), multi-layer pipeline (syntax→security→coverage→architecture), semantic/behavioral analysis, SDLC-wide placement, escape valves with documented overrides.

5. CHROMADB: Batch 50-250 docs, avoid concurrent embedded access (use client-server), memory formula N=RAM_GB*0.245M, rebuild HNSW for SIMD (3-5x query speed), reduce embedding dimensionality.

Key actionable items for Torus: (a) Implement agent contracts for resource budgeting, (b) Add async PostToolUse hooks for non-blocking validation, (c) Deploy ChromaDB in client-server mode, (d) Implement GEPA prompt evolution, (e) Add maker-checker pattern for critical edits.

Multi-Instance Agent Architecture (codename TBD, inspired by Gastown comparison). Core concept: persistent Claude Code sessions running as named role-agents (researcher-alpha, builder, planner, etc.) coordinated via file-based IPC through ~/.claude/channels/. Key design decisions so far:
1. Role-named, not model-named (researcher-alpha not researcher-opus) — model is a launch flag, role is identity
2. Shared memory MCP with agent provenance tags (agent:researcher-alpha, model:opus)
3. File-based coordination: task_{role}.json (inbox), result_{role}.json (outbox), log.jsonl (broadcast)
4. Shell watcher scripts bridge task files → tmux send-keys (Claude can't poll files)
5. Worktrees for code-editing roles (builder, debugger), normal launch for read-only roles (researcher, planner, verifier)
6. Gate 13 needs "co-claim" concept for collaborative file editing
7. Persistent instances preferred over on-demand (no idle billing, warm context)
8. Main instance can /compact or /clear workers via same watcher channel
9. Gastown comparison: Gastown scales agents via git-backed state, Torus makes each agent smarter via cognitive memory. Combined = best of both.

Trading framework — final design decisions summary (Session 343):

PROJECT: "Trading Bot" (placeholder name), located at ~/projects/trading-framework/. Separate standalone Python project, built using torus, runs independently.

ARCHITECTURE: Connector pattern (multi-asset), scheduler abstraction (multi-timeframe), embedded Python gates (<1ms), graduated autonomy (paper → real).

DECISIONS:
- Asset class: All via connectors, crypto (Binance/CCXT) first
- Timeframe: All via schedulers, daily/swing first
- Execution: Paper + Full Auto start, graduate through 5 levels

- Backtesting: Build our own (for-loop over candles using PaperConnector + gate runner, no Backtrader/Zipline)

LIBRARIES (use, don't build):

BUILD (don't use existing):
- Engine core, gate system, strategy framework, backtester, journal

PHASES: 1) Foundation + Paper + 4 gates + 1 strategy. 2) Binance connector + real data. 3) Second asset class. 4) Claude analysis + Claude strategy (paper). 5) Graduate to real money.

Data models, strategy interface, journal schema — implementation details, not blocking decisions. Will be defined during building.

Trading framework build plan — 4 phases:

Phase 1 (Foundation): Connector abstract interface, data models (Order, Position, Price, Candle), PaperConnector (simulated, no real money), engine core (strategy runner, gate checks, journal), 2-3 initial gates (risk check, position limit, daily loss). No real broker needed.

Phase 2 (First real connector): BinanceConnector via CCXT for crypto. Test paper vs real with same strategy. One simple strategy (e.g., moving average crossover).

Phase 3 (Second asset class): OandaConnector (forex) or AlpacaConnector (stocks). Zero engine changes — just a new connector file. Validate same strategy works across asset classes.

Phase 4 (Intelligence): Claude-in-the-loop for research/thesis (PTC for efficiency). Memory integration for trade outcome learning. More gates (correlation, cooldown, backtest proof). Performance analytics.

Key decision: Design interface for all asset classes from day one, implement one connector at a time. Abstraction costs ~1.5 hours upfront vs 8-14+ hours to retrofit later. Start with PaperConnector, then crypto (Binance/CCXT — 24/7, no PDT rules, testable with $10), then stocks/forex.

Architecture: Strategy/gates/journal are asset-agnostic (work in percentages). Only the connector layer knows about specific brokers. Engine never imports broker libraries directly.

Trading framework gate system — 12 gates across 3 tiers, detailed design:

TIER 1 (Hard block, fail-closed, no override):
- Gate 1: Position Size — blocks if single position > 5% of portfolio
- Gate 2: Daily Loss Limit — halts all trading if daily P&L exceeds -3%
- Gate 3: Insufficient Balance — blocks if can't afford order (accounts for reserved capital)
- Gate 4: Market Closed — blocks if market not open (crypto always open, stocks/forex have hours)

TIER 2 (Block with override, fail-open):
- Gate 5: Thesis Required (Phase 4) — blocks if no thesis logged, override with explicit reason
- Gate 6: Cooldown — blocks after 3 consecutive losses, 30min cooldown
- Gate 7: Max Open Positions — blocks at 5 simultaneous positions
- Gate 8: Correlation Guard (Phase 3) — blocks if new position correlation >0.7 with existing
- Gate 9: Backtest Proof (Phase 4) — blocks if strategy has no backtest results

TIER 3 (Advisory, trade proceeds, warning logged):
- Gate 10: Unusual Size — warns if order >2x average order size
- Gate 11: Off-Hours — warns if trading outside configured active hours
- Gate 12: Strategy Underperforming — warns if win rate <40% over last 20 trades

Gate runner: sorted by tier, T1/T2 block execution, T3 logs only. Fail-closed gates block on crash, fail-open gates log error and continue. All thresholds configurable via settings.json.

Phase 1 builds: gates 1,2,3,4,6,7 (capital protection + basic discipline). Gates 5,8,9 in later phases. Gates 10,11,12 in Phase 2.

Worker Service & Persistent Worker Discussion (Session 24):

WHAT IT IS: A persistent HTTP daemon (NOT an agent) that runs on localhost:37777 as a background OS process (like nginx or postgres). It stays running for the entire Claude Code session, keeping database connections open so memory queries are fast (~50ms) instead of cold-starting each time (~500-1000ms).

OUR SYSTEM (cold-start MCP): Each memory tool call starts a new process → opens DB → queries → returns → dies. Next call repeats everything. Isolated but slow on repeated calls.

CLAUDE-MEM'S SYSTEM (persistent worker): Boots once at session start → keeps DB connections open → serves all memory requests via HTTP → shuts down at session end. Fast on repeated calls but single point of failure.

KEY DISTINCTION: The worker is NOT an agent, NOT an LLM, NOT using tokens. It's a regular server process (Bun/Node.js runtime) that manages the database. It has zero impact on token context or API costs. Only the MCP tool RESULTS (what gets returned to Claude) cost tokens — and those are the same size regardless of whether the backend is cold-start or persistent.

WEB UI: React app served by the worker at localhost:37777. Shows live feed of observations, summaries, prompts via SSE. Only visualizes memory — doesn't know about gates, audit, skills, agents. Our framework has far more data worth dashboarding.

RECOMMENDATION: Keep our cold-start MCP (reliable, working). Build our own lightweight dashboard that shows ALL our data (gates, audit, memories, agents, test status). Consider persistent worker only if latency becomes a measurable problem.

VOICE HANDLING:

- One-time setup: Sends instruction to Claude to append "END_TORUS_RESPONSE" to every response

- Extraction: Finds marker, skips user message line, captures until sentinel

OptimisticChain genesis storage slots computed and embedded (2026-02-10):

CREATED: /home/crab/OptimisticChain/contracts/script/ComputeGenesisStorage.s.sol
- Foundry script that deploys all 6 contracts with constructor args, reads storage via vm.load(), outputs slot→value pairs
- Run with: forge script script/ComputeGenesisStorage.s.sol:ComputeGenesisStorage -vvv

UPDATED: /home/crab/OptimisticChain/devnet/scripts/generate-genesis.sh
- Now embeds "storage" objects alongside "code" and "balance" for each predeploy

GENESIS STORAGE SUMMARY (27 non-zero slots total):
1. StakingManager (0x...0100): 3 slots - governor, minValidatorStake=32 ether, stakingAPYBps=500
2. PassiveStaking (0x...0101): 11 slots - governor + 6 tier configs (lockDuration + packed apyBps|voteMultiplier per tier). Mapping slots at keccak256(abi.encode(tierIndex, 1))
3. FeeDistributor (0x...0102): 3 slots - startShares (packed 4x uint16), endShares (packed 4x uint16), totalTransitionEpochs=1825
4. ChainGovernor (0x...0103): 4 slots - quorumBps=400, votingPeriod=50400, executionDelay=7200, permanentUnlockThresholdBps=8000
5. Treasury (0x...0104): 0 slots (no constructor storage, uses Predeploys constants in bytecode)
6. RewardMinter (0x...0105): 6 slots - epochLength=600, stakingManager addr, passiveStaking addr, governor, stakingAPYBps=500, passiveAPYBps=100

KEY INSIGHT: PassiveStaking tier configs are stored in a mapping(uint8 => TierConfig) at slot 1. Each TierConfig spans 2 storage words. The mapping key hash is keccak256(abi.encode(uint256(tierIndex), uint256(1))). Tier 0 and 5 have lockDuration=0 (tier 0 = no lock, tier 5 = permanent), so those lockDuration slots are omitted from genesis (zero default).

FeeDistributor's FeeShares struct packs 4 x uint16 into one 32-byte word: burnBps | (validatorBps << 16) | (treasuryBps << 32) | (dappBps << 48).

## Multi-Agent Coordination: Swarm Intelligence Patterns (arxiv research, session268)

### Key Papers:

**SwarmAgentic** (arxiv:2506.15672) - PSO-Inspired Agentic System Generation
- Constructs agentic systems from scratch, jointly optimizes agent functionality + collaboration
- Maintains population of candidate systems, evolves via feedback-guided updates
- Inspired by Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) — maintain population, evolve best performers

**SwarmSys** (arxiv:2510.10047) - Decentralized Swarm-Inspired Reasoning
- 3 specialized roles: Explorers, Workers, Validators
- Coordination emerges through iterative interactions (not centralized control)
- Closed-loop framework for distributed multi-agent reasoning
- Maps to Torus's 6 agent types: researcher=Explorer, builder=Worker, stress-tester=Validator

**SIER** (arxiv:2505.17115) - Swarm Intelligence Enhanced Reasoning
- Formulates LLM reasoning as optimization problem
- Density-driven framework: group of LLM agents collaboratively search for optimal solutions

**Society of HiveMind** (arxiv:2503.05473)
- Orchestrates interactions between multiple foundation models
- Imitates animal swarm behavior + modern evolutionary theories

### Pattern for Torus:
- Explorer/Worker/Validator triad maps cleanly to research→build→test pipeline
- Population-based approach: run N agent instances, keep best outputs (PSO-inspired)

Hive Mind — HuggingFace/GitHub/GitLab as memory layer research:

HF Hub (best for vector search): Lance format (Feb 2026) enables HNSW index stored on Hub — sub-second vector search without downloading. DuckDB+VSS for SQL+vector queries over parquet. Spaces as query API. Rate limits: 1K-6K API calls/5min depending on plan. Storage: 100GB private free, $18/TB/month extra. Batch-only writes (push_to_hub commits).

GitHub (best for provenance/review): CODEOWNERS for domain experts, Actions for LLM quality gates, PR review workflow. Copilot Agentic Memory (Jan 2026) already uses repo-as-memory pattern with citation verification. Push limit: 6/min/repo (~360 commits/hr). No native vector search — keyword only via Search API. 5K req/hr authenticated.

GitLab (best self-hosted): CE is free + unlimited storage/CI with own runners. Knowledge Graph (beta v18.4) for semantic code search. 7.2K req/hr API limit (configurable on self-hosted). Duo Agent Platform for custom agents.

Recommendation: HF Hub with Lance as warm layer (semantic search) + local LanceDB as hot cache + GitHub for review workflow (CODEOWNERS + Actions). GitLab self-hosted for private/high-throughput deployments. None support real-time writes — all batch/commit oriented, hybrid with local DB is mandatory for real-time.

Hive Mind Option — Telegram as knowledge layer research:

CRITICAL FINDING: Bots cannot see messages from other bots in Telegram groups. The organizer MUST run as MTProto service account (Pyrogram/Telethon), not a standard bot.

Architecture: Supergroup (forum mode) as working group + linked Verified Channel (organizer posts promoted items). Forum topics = knowledge domains (up to 1M topics). Agent bots post structured JSON with hashtags. Organizer indexes into local LanceDB, handles dedup/quality/pruning, responds to inline queries for search.

Rate limits: 20 msg/min per bot per group, 30 msg/s global per token, 20 bots per group max. File upload: 50MB (bot API), 2GB (MTProto). Message limit: 4096 chars.

Unique value: FREE permanent server-side storage (Telegram pays), human-readable audit trail, reactions as quality signals, forum topics as domains, built-in ACL via group membership, dual-use notification channel.

Dealbreakers: No E2E encryption in groups (Telegram sees everything), 20-bot limit per group, MTProto service account may violate ToS, no native semantic search (must maintain external index), Telegram as SPOF.

Best for: Non-sensitive knowledge, <20 agent bots, async workflows, human oversight wanted. Not for: sensitive data, high throughput, large swarms.

LanceDB migration detailed research (Session 231): memory_server.py has 5 ChromaDB collections (knowledge, fix_outcomes, observations, web_pages, quarantine), uses nomic-embed-text-v2-moe (768-dim), lazy init via _ensure_initialized(). LanceDB v0.29.x API: connect(), create_table(schema=pyarrow), search().distance_type("cosine").limit(N), merge_insert("id") for upsert, SQL-style WHERE filters, native BM25 FTS via create_fts_index(). Key differences: metadata becomes typed Arrow columns (not dict), upsert is merge_insert pattern, delete uses SQL predicates, manual optimize() needed for compaction. Concurrent access uses optimistic CC (better than ChromaDB segfaults but still needs single-writer UDS gateway).

## Added to Reliability section:
- **Agent auto-compaction**: Monitor worker agent context usage (token estimate or tool call count). When threshold hit, main sends /compact to the worker to summarize and free context. Different from resource budgets (which cap duration) — compaction extends useful lifetime. Could trigger via heartbeat metadata (agent reports context % used) or via watcher polling tool_call_count from state files.

## Added to Coordination section:
- **Async delegation (fire-and-forget)**: Main should be able to dispatch a task and continue working without blocking. When result arrives, main gets notified (similar to background Agent tool behavior). Current v1 is effectively sync — main pastes prompt then waits for result file. Async mode: dispatch → continue → get callback/notification on result. Implementation options: (a) watcher writes to a notification channel that main polls lazily, (b) hook on main's PreToolUse checks for completed results and injects notification, (c) dedicated MCP tool that checks result status.

## Arxiv Survey: Actionable Patterns for Torus Framework Memory System (session268)

Based on 2025-2026 arxiv research, these patterns are directly applicable to our LanceDB + nomic embeddings system:

### 1. Hybrid Importance Score (MemOS pattern)
```
score = α*cosine_similarity + β*recency(last_access) + γ*access_frequency - δ*age_decay
```
- Add `access_count` and `last_accessed_at` fields to LanceDB knowledge table
- Use score for retrieval ranking beyond pure cosine similarity

### 2. Memory Lifecycle States (MemOS)
Active → Archived → Expired pipeline
- Entries below importance threshold → Archived (kept, deprioritized)
- Very old + low access → Expired (candidates for pruning)
- Compactor should use lifecycle states, not just recency

### 3. Adaptive Forgetting (MemoryBank/FadeMem)
- Implement Ebbinghaus-inspired decay: strength = e^(-t/S) where S = stability factor
- High-priority tags (critical, high) = higher S (slower decay)
- Low-priority or untagged = lower S (faster decay)

### 4. Memory Consolidation (TeleMem pattern)
- Batch similar memories → cluster → LLM-merge into single consolidated entry
- memory_compactor.py could implement this as "semantic clustering consolidation"

### 5. Structured Memory with Rich Metadata (A-MEM/Zettelkasten)
- Each memory: contextual description + keywords + tags + linked memory IDs
- Cross-linking: when storing, find related memories and add bidirectional links
- Enables graph traversal during retrieval (not just similarity search)

### 6. Adaptive Structure Selection (FluxMem, arXiv 2602.14038 — Feb 2026)
- Different interactions warrant different memory structures (flat, graph, episodic)
- Train/learn which structure works best per interaction type
- Could inform Torus 3-tier classification improvements

### 7. RL-Based Memory Operations (Memory-R1)
- Explicit ADD/UPDATE/DELETE/NOOP decisions by Memory Manager agent
- Could replace heuristic remember_this() with policy-driven storage decisions

Gate 19 (Hindsight) toggle investigation (Session 325):

ISSUE: Gate 19 appears toggled off but may still block edits despite display saying it's off.

KEY FINDINGS:
1. Gate 19 toggle mechanism is TWO-PART:
   - Primary toggle: "mentor_hindsight_gate" (individual)
   - Override toggle: "mentor_all" (master switch)
   
2. get_live_toggle() logic (shared/state.py:656):
   - Checks config.json FIRST (canonical location)
   - Falls back to LIVE_STATE.json for backward compat
   - Caches results per process — doesn't re-read files
   
3. Gate 19 check logic (gate_19_hindsight.py:46):
   ```python
   if not (get_live_toggle("mentor_hindsight_gate") or get_live_toggle("mentor_all")):
       return GateResult(blocked=False)
   ```
   This means: Gate runs if EITHER toggle is true. ONE true = runs.
   
4. Current state in config.json:
   - mentor_hindsight_gate: NOT SET (missing = False via .get(key, None) → None)
   - mentor_all: TRUE (explicitly set in config.json line 15)
   
5. Circuit breaker state (.circuit_breaker_state.json):
   - gate_19_hindsight: state="CLOSED", total_crashes=0, total_skips=0
   - NOT open or broken — operates normally
   
6. Statusline display (statusline.py:1057):
   - Shows "Hindsight-Gate 19" toggle status via _tog('mentor_hindsight_gate')
   - Only shows mentor_hindsight_gate toggle, NOT the mentor_all override
   - This creates UI blind spot: mentor_all can activate gate while display says off
   
7. Test expectation (test_analytics.py:464):
   - Tests expect mentor_hindsight_gate to be False
   - But tests pass because mentor_all is True
   
ROOT CAUSE: mentor_all=true in config.json activates Gate 19 regardless of mentor_hindsight_gate toggle. Statusline only displays mentor_hindsight_gate, hiding the mentor_all override.

Session 88: Implemented FTS5 thread safety and query cap fixes in memory_server.py.

Fix 1 - Thread Lock: Added threading.Lock() to FTS5Index class. Wrapped keyword_search(), tag_search(), add_entry(), and get_preview() with self._lock. Used check_same_thread=False on sqlite3.connect(). Init-time methods (is_synced, build_from_chromadb, reset_and_rebuild) left unlocked since they run single-threaded before UDS gateway starts. Prevents "database is locked" errors when MCP tool calls and UDS gateway queries hit FTS5 simultaneously.

Fix 2 - Query Cap: Added 5000-char cap at top of _sanitize_fts_query(). Prevents pathological memory/CPU usage on oversized queries. 5000 chosen as balance between BM25 search quality (shorter = better ranking) and completeness (never truncates real queries). Safety margin: 20x below the ~100KB danger zone.

Test count: 1004 → 1005 (all passing).

FTS5 Persistence Analysis (Session 22): Full trade-off analysis completed. Key findings: (1) BENEFITS: faster cold start at scale (26ms now → ~650ms at 5000 entries vs ~2ms persistent), future-proofing, reduced ChromaDB load on restart. (2) RISKS: staleness/desync if ChromaDB changes externally (high), crash corruption (medium), complexity increase for sync validation + crash recovery (medium). (3) ZERO token/context impact — FTS5 is entirely server-side. (4) You lose the "always perfectly in sync" guarantee that in-memory rebuild provides. (5) Dual-write pattern already in place (line 773-774) — hardest part done. (6) DECISION: Hold at current threshold — revisit at ~800 memories. Currently at 195, rebuild is 26ms (imperceptible). The crossover where persistence is worth the complexity is ~800-1000 entries.

## Arxiv Survey: Memory Consolidation Patterns for LLM Agents (2025-2026)

### TeleMem (arXiv 2601.06037)
- Organizes memory as **structured semantic trajectories** (not isolated fragments)
- Pipeline: batch summaries → retrieve related memories → cluster semantically aligned entries → LLM-based consolidation → commit to persistent storage
- Enables dependency-aware reasoning and consistent context restoration

### Mem0 (arXiv 2504.19413)
- Production-ready: dynamically extracts, consolidates, and retrieves salient info from conversations
- Outperforms all baselines on single-hop, temporal, multi-hop, open-domain QA
- 26% accuracy boost over baseline memory systems

### AgeMem (arXiv 2601.01885, Jan 2026)
- **Unified LTM + STM management** in a single agent policy
- Memory operations exposed as **tool-based actions**: store, retrieve, update, summarize, discard
- Agent autonomously decides what/when to store — no heuristics

### A-MEM (arXiv 2502.12110)
- **Zettelkasten method**: creates interconnected knowledge networks
- Each new memory gets: contextual description, keywords, tags, dynamic indexing
- Enables effective capture of information relationships

Session 170 Deep Investigation: Why subagents get stuck creating Memory-Architecture-Diagram.html

ROOT CAUSE: Gate 4 (memory-first) staleness loop. Three interacting mechanisms:

1. GATE 4 FRESHNESS WINDOW = 300 seconds (5 min). Gated tools: Edit, Write, NotebookEdit, Task.
2. SIDEBAND FILE (.memory_last_queried) is SHARED across all sessions, written ONCE at boot (SessionStart). Silent failure on write errors.
3. SUBAGENT STATE ISOLATION: Each subagent gets fresh state with memory_last_queried=0. Relies entirely on shared sideband file.

THE LOOP:
- Subagent boots → sideband written with timestamp
- Subagent researches codebase (reads files, searches) → takes 3-4 minutes
- Subagent issues Write for large HTML → Gate 4 checks sideband
- If boot timestamp > 5 min old → BLOCKED
- Subagent queries memory (resets sideband) → starts research again → same cycle

COMPOUNDING FACTORS:
- Gate 10 blocks subagents without explicit model parameter (wastes a turn)
- Enforcer gate cache TTL = 60 seconds (doesn't help for 5-min window)
- Gate 7 has even stricter 3-minute window for critical files
- Session 143 had identical problem with architecture doc

EXISTING FILES: ~/Desktop/ has torus-architecture-diagram-NEW.html (44K), torus-architecture-NEW.html (56K), etc. — eventually created after multiple retries.

POTENTIAL FIXES:
1. Refresh sideband on MCP memory tool calls (not just boot)
2. Increase Gate 4 freshness window for Write tool (or exempt new file creation)
3. Have subagent boot refresh sideband independently
4. Allow gate 4 tuning via state override for long-write scenarios

Session 171: Implemented Gate 4 Staleness Loop Fix (F1+F2e+F3+F4) — 4 complementary fixes:

F1 (orchestrator.py:127-133): Redundant sideband write on every MCP memory tool PostToolUse call. Calls _write_sideband_timestamp() after setting state["memory_last_queried"], keeping sideband fresh for all sessions.

F2e (gate_04_memory_first.py:76-82): Write to non-existent file (new file creation) is exempt from staleness window IF get_memory_last_queried(state) > 0. If memory was never queried, falls through to normal block. Prevents staleness loop for subagents creating large HTML/docs.

F3 (gate_04_memory_first.py:23,88-90): Per-tool freshness windows. WRITE_FRESHNESS_WINDOW = 600 seconds (10 min) vs default MEMORY_FRESHNESS_WINDOW = 300 (5 min). Edit stays at 5 min. gate_tune_overrides still works (overrides whichever base window applies).

F4 (enforcer.py:599-613): Subagent mini-boot — refreshes sideband on first PreToolUse of a new session_id, but ONLY for UUID-pattern session IDs (real subagent sessions). Uses len >= 8, dash at position 8, hex chars in first 8 positions. Test sessions excluded.

CRITICAL TEST FIX: Direct gate 4 check() tests need sideband cleanup (os.remove(MEMORY_TIMESTAMP_FILE)) before the test block, because get_memory_last_queried() always checks the global sideband file. Added cleanup before both the auto-tune test block and the new staleness test block.

Feature #4 PMI Filtering implementation in progress (Toroidal-teams branch, 2026-03-11):

CHANGES TO knowledge_graph.py:
1. _create_tables(): Added fail-open migration to add co_occurrence_count (INTEGER DEFAULT 0) and pmi (REAL, NULL=not computed) columns to edges table.
2. add_edge(): Updated INSERT to include co_occurrence_count=1 and ON CONFLICT increments co_occurrence_count+1.
3. update_pmi(): New method after edge_count(). Computes PMI = log2(co_count * total_memories / (mention_x * mention_y)), stores to edges.pmi. Skips if total_memories<2 or counts are zero. Fail-open (except: pass).
4. _get_neighbors(): Now selects pmi and co_occurrence_count. Returns PMI-weighted effective strength: legacy edges (co_count=0 or pmi=None) use raw strength; pmi>0 => strength*pmi; pmi<=0 => 0.0 (blocked).

CHANGES TO memory_server.py:
1. Added top-level import math (line ~20).
2. Added _tag_total_memories: int = 0 global after _tag_cooccurrence_dirty.
3. _build_tag_cooccurrence(): global declaration updated to include _tag_total_memories; sets _tag_total_memories = len(mem_tags) after _tag_counts = tag_totals.
4. _get_expanded_tags(): Replaced 40% rate threshold with PMI > 1.0 check: tag_pmi = math.log2((co_count * N) / (count_x * count_y)).
5. remember_this(): After add_edge() calls, now also calls _knowledge_graph.update_pmi(e1, e2, "co_occurs", _kg_total) where _kg_total = collection.count().
